title: SaVeR Optimal Data Collection Strategy for Safe Policy Evaluation in Tabular MDP
abstract: In this paper, we study safe data collection for the purpose of policy evaluation in tabular Markov decision processes (MDPs). In policy evaluation, we are given a target policy and asked to estimate the expected cumulative reward it will obtain. Policy evaluation requires data and we are interested in the question of what behavior policy should collect the data for the most accurate evaluation of the target policy. While prior work has considered behavior policy selection, in this paper, we additionally consider a safety constraint on the behavior policy. Namely, we assume there exists a known default policy that incurs a particular expected cost when run and we enforce that the cumulative cost of all behavior policies ran is better than a constant factor of the cost that would be incurred had we always run the default policy. We first show that there exists a class of intractable MDPs where no safe oracle algorithm with knowledge about problem parameters can efficiently collect data and satisfy the safety constraints. We then define the tractability condition for an MDP such that a safe oracle algorithm can efficiently collect data and using that we prove the first lower bound for this setting. We then introduce an algorithm SaVeR for this problem that approximates the safe oracle algorithm and bound the finite-sample mean squared error of the algorithm while ensuring it satisfies the safety constraint. Finally, we show in simulations that SaVeR produces low MSE policy evaluation while satisfying the safety constraint.
impact: In this paper, we study the safe data collection for policy evaluation in an RL setting under safety constraints. Our paper proposes a new adaptive data collection policy and addresses the theoretical challenges posed by this setting. We focus on algorithmic and theoretical contributions and we do not address the challenges that might stem from incorrect feedback, human bias in feedback, false information, or social disparity in gathering the feedback (or dataset). We therefore leave it to the users who apply our algorithm to use it responsibly and ethically.

query_input.scenario: Energy grid control policy off-policy evaluation with blackout risk constraint
query_input.issue: Tractability conditions unmet in large state spaces can make MSE lower bounds vacuous, masking true risk.
hazard: Off-policy safety-cost estimates with finite-sample MSE guarantees that are sensitive to the data collection strategy, enabling overconfident certification of unsafe policies.
exposure: Grid operators relying on safety reports to authorize new load-shedding control strategies.

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Grid operators lack means to detect when tractability conditions fail, causing trust in vacuous finite-sample safety guarantees.
hipporag_result.impact: Authorized unsafe load-shedding strategies trigger cascading blackouts, compromising grid stability and public safety.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Off-policy safety-cost estimates with finite-sample MSE guarantees, through grid operators relying on safety reports to authorize load-shedding strategies, exploit undetected tractability failures, causing trust in vacuous guarantees, authorized unsafe strategies that trigger cascading blackouts, compromising grid stability and public safety.

dose_response: Overconfident vacuous safety bounds enable unjustified trust in load-shedding policies, which in grid control exposes operators' reliance on flawed reports, leading to large-scale blackouts and social unrest.
vulnerability: Grid operators treat vacuous lower bounds as meaningful, leading to unjustified trust in load-shedding policies.
impact: Large-scale blackouts disrupt healthcare, transportation, and communications, causing economic losses and social unrest.
